title: Not everything is an h1
checkLabels.t4: Why I like it came down to h2
why.empty: The editor is empty. You deleted the page instead of fixing it. Bring the code back and change the levels — not with deletion.
why.not-closed: A tag is open and never closed, or closed in the wrong order.
hint2.not-closed: It is almost always a forgotten closing tag. You change the opening one and the closing one stays behind.
hint3.not-closed:
Contact

looks innocent. It is not. You open one thing and close another — the browser does not know what to do with that.
hint4.not-closed: Go through the headings one by one. For each, check that the number at the front and the number at the back are the same.
why.no-h1: There is no main heading, or it is empty. One <h1> must remain — it says what the WHOLE page is about.
hint2.no-h1: You brought all three down. One of them is not a section — it is the topic.
hint3.no-h1: A book with no title on the cover. You open it and Chapter One starts right away. You do not know what you are holding.
hint4.no-h1: Put back as <h1> the heading that describes the whole page. The other two stay <h2>.
why.still-h1: The page has more than one <h1>. If everything is an h1, it shouts equally loudly about everything — and both Google and the blind reader get lost in it.
hint2.still-h1: Count the h1 tags in your code. Exactly one must remain — the one that describes the whole page.
hint3.still-h1: Think of a book's contents: one chapter called Insects, with Feeding and Breeding inside it. There is one chapter. There can be as many sections as you like.
hint4.still-h1: Which of the three headings describes the whole page? That one stays an h1. The other two are sections inside it.
why.no-h2: Why I like it is still not a sub-heading. It is not what the whole page is about — it is a section inside it. Go down one level: <h2>.
hint2.no-h2: The heading Why I like it has an opening tag and a closing tag. Both are h1.
hint3.no-h2:
If you have

Contact

and you want it to be a section, you write

Contact

. Change the one to a two at the front — and at the back too.
hint4.no-h2: Change both tags around Why I like it. Forget the closing one and the everything-closed check will fail.
why.no-h2-second: You brought the first one down. Not the second. How I started is a section too, not the topic of the page.
hint2.no-h2-second: Do to How I started exactly what you did to Why I like it. Both tags, not just one.
why.lost-p: A paragraph is gone. The task is to change the heading levels, not to delete text. Bring it back.
hint2.lost-p: Look at the preview. The sentences It never gets boring and A friend showed it to me must still be there.
blocks.0.text: Look at the page on the right. Three headings, all equally large. It looks... wrong, does it not? Like a person shouting the whole time.
blocks.1.text: The problem is not aesthetic. The problem is that the page has no hierarchy — you cannot tell what is the main thing and what is a subsection.
blocks.4.text: From <h1> to <h6>. Like a book's contents: one chapter, sections inside, subsections inside those.
blocks.6.text: One <h1> per page — it says what the whole page is about. Everything below it is <h2>, and subsections of <h2> are <h3>. Do not skip levels.
blocks.7.text: And most importantly: you do not choose the level by how large you want it to look. You choose it by how important the thing is. The size is a consequence, not a goal.
blocks.8.text: Fix the page on the right: leave one <h1> and make the other two <h2>.
blocks.9.text: Google reads your headings to understand what the page is about. Blind people use them to jump around it. If everything is an h1, both get lost.
